responseSet.documentRefHTML: Murphy, T. (1991) Psychoadynamic Psychiatry By John Frosch 2 volumes. International Universities Press, vol. I $50 Pp. xxi + 359; vol. II $60 Pp. xiv + 503.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:274-275
responseSet.documentRefXML: Murphy, T. (1991) Psychoadynamic Psychiatry By John Frosch 2 volumes. International Universities Press, vol. I $50 Pp. xxi + 359; vol. II $60 Pp. xiv + 503.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:274-275
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 3 274-275 Psychoadynamic Psychiatry By John Frosch 2 volumes. International Universities Press, vol. I $50 Pp. xxi + 359; vol. II $60 Pp. xiv + 503. Tom Murphy
responseSet.title: Psychoadynamic Psychiatry By John Frosch 2 volumes. International Universities Press, vol. I $50 Pp. xxi + 359; vol. II $60 Pp. xiv + 503.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoadynamic Psychiatry By John Frosch2 volumes. International Universities Press, vol. I $50 Pp. xxi + 359; vol. II $60 Pp. xiv + 503.

Tom Murphy

Tom Murphy

Tom Murphy  

As one would expect from this author, these two volumes provide a thorough exposition of the psychoanalytic perspective on the psychiatric field, from a mainly North American Ego Psychology viewpoint.

274

Volume I covers psychic development from infancy to old age; psychiatric conditions by diagnosis are tackled towards the end of Volume I and for most of Volume II; principles of psychodynamic psychotherapy provide the final chapter of Volume II. Organisational/institutional dynamics are not included. Comprehensive reviews of other published work (mainly USA) are included with each topic, alongside the author's well articulated and considered views.

In general the book feels quite condensed, although the style is light and more readable than I had expected. DSM III R categories are used as the diagnostic framework, and this is particularly helpful in the section on psychosis, avoiding the trap of referring to all psychotic manifestations as ‘Schizophrenia’.

The Object relations viewpoint is included in the earlier sections and referred to at times later on. The impression given, however, is that this was for the sake of completeness as this approach is clearly considered to be of minor relevance compared to the ‘classical’ one. I felt this theoretical divergence most acutely in the section on Psychosis, where the sufferer from schizophrenia is seen as lacking object relations, rather than living with acute sensitivity in a world of primitive object relations.

The author makes it explicit at the outset that this is ‘not a textbook on psychodynamic psychiatry’, but is concerned with principles. As a consequence, somewhat more space is given to theory than to clinical material, with the latter mainly used to illustrate conclusions rather than pose questions.

In summary, more of a reference book than for reading straight through, giving a comprehensive overview of current thinking, mainly from a North American viewpoint, on psychiatric conditions.

275

responseSet.title: Running on the Spot or Can Nursing Really Change?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Running on the Spot or Can Nursing Really Change?**

Julia Fabricius

Julia Fabricius

Minerva House Chenies Street London WC1E 7ER

Julia Fabricius  

In this paper, the nature of the psychotherapeutic function of the general nurse is considered in the light of current and future changes in nursing. Important ways in which general nurses can be psychotherapeutic in the course of their everyday work are highlighted and linked with the maternal function. The main thesis is that many apparently progressive changes in nursing occur in such a way that there is no real change in the organisational defences which are used to prevent therapeutic closeness with patients. Possible reasons for this are suggested, in particular that the defences serve to prevent distressing projections from patients spreading up through the organisational hierarchy. This in turn leaves the nurses ‘at the bedside’ without the containment they need in order to tolerate these projections. The denigration by nurses of the maternal function is also considered and it is suggested that this not only plays a part in the defence but also, paradoxically, gives nurses less status and recognition rather than the greater status they seek.

responseSet.title: Promoting Psychoanalytic Understanding in the Core Professions: The Case against Establishing a new Profession of Adult Psychotherapist
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Promoting Psychoanalytic Understanding in the Core Professions: The Case against Establishing a new Profession of Adult Psychotherapist

Phil Mollon PHD

Phil Mollon

Stevenage, Herts. SG1 4AB

Phil Mollon  

Currently there is much enthusiastic talk about the possibility of establishing a new National Health Service profession of Adult Psychotherapist. This paper questions the assumption that such a development would be rational and advantageous. Four main points are discussed here: there are problems arising from training in a uni-dimensional context when returning to work in a multidimensional context; there are dangers in taking a method-oriented stance rather than one which is service-oriented; there is something peculiar about the notion of a profession based on a single method or mode of treatment; one of the most important contributions of psychoanalysis to the core professions is the creation of an analytic space for thinking.

responseSet.title: Running on the Spot or can Nursing Really Change?: Response to Julia Fabricius
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Running on the Spot or can Nursing Really Change?: Response to Julia Fabricius**

Michael Conran

Michael Conran

29 Hamilton Gardens London NW8 9PU

Michael Conran  

It May Be Argued That a doctor has no business addressing the subject of Nursing and its related problems. Moreover, Julia Fabricius has, you will have noticed, made little reference to doctors.

My first inclination was to hang my head and apologise for a medical intrusion; or at least to make some deferential mumblings. But further reflection inclines me less to such pussy-footing.

Clemenceau declared war to be too serious a matter to be left to generals. I venture to say that Nursing may, likewise, be too serious to be left to nurses in isolation. Much the same could be said of doctors who, as we know, frequently have difficulty in hearing that which impinges upon maternal feeling, whether from within themselves, from nurses, patients, or anyone else.

Nevertheless, as a psychoanalyst who is also a doctor of medicine, I must assure you that I am immensely proud of my medical qualification. Moreover, I have long held the view, first expressed by Claude Bernard, the founder of Experimental Medicine, in the 1850s, that isolation leads to annihilation, and that only in the relationship between entities is scientific truth to be found. We therefore think of a man not in splendid isolation but in relationship to himself, to his objects, to women, to children, to peers, to society, and so forth. Consequently I see myself at this Conference neither as an autonomous fount of wisdom nor as a fount of foolishness; but as in relationship to all those listed, and especially in this context to nurses generally and Julia Fabricius in particular.

For I must tell you plainly that just as I personally owe an immeasurable professional debt to certain nurses — such that I speak of the relationship in no theoretical way — so I say that as long as we doctors and nurses abjure or deny our interdependence we are all in dire trouble. Within three months — or was it weeks? — of pretentious arrogance as a junior psychiatrist, I finally turned to a charge nurse and said ‘I don't know what I am doing. Will you help me?’ This nurse closed the office door, put his back to it, and told me, in very plain English, what he thought of me. I was not flattered. Thereafter, he, and other nurses too, came to show me just exactly what a nurse could bring to a very sick and helpless individual.

responseSet.title: Object Relations Group Psychotherapy: The Group as an Object, a Tool, and a Training Base By Ramon Ganzarain Madison: Int. Univ. Press. $40.00. Pp. 383.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Relations Group Psychotherapy: The Group as an Object, a Tool, and a Training Base By Ramon Ganzarain Madison: Int. Univ. Press. $40.00. Pp. 383.

Margaret Malcolm

Margaret Malcolm

Margaret Malcolm  

In this book Ramon Ganzarain describes some of his thirty years' experience of working in both therapeutic and training groups. Many of the chapters have been

172

published elsewhere. This probably accounts for a rather ‘dated’ feel to some of the chapters, and especially in the section on training, a lack of cohesiveness in sticking to his theme of object relations and group psychotherapy.

Despite these criticisms, it is a lively book, full of rich clinical material and a clear exposition of Kleinian concepts and their relevance to primitive group phenomena. The second section of the book concentrates on technique and highlights the difficulties of working with particular patient groups, including those who have been sexually abused.

I would recommend this book to the beginning group psychotherapist. The theory is lucid and the richness of the clinical material illustrates the trials and rewards of groups in a way which the new therapeutic would find supportive.

173

responseSet.documentRefHTML: Gore, V. (1991) Through the Night: Helping Parents and Sleepless Infants. By Dylis Dawsa London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 paperback. 274 Pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:173-174
responseSet.documentRefXML: Gore, V. (1991) Through the Night: Helping Parents and Sleepless Infants. By Dylis Dawsa London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 paperback. 274 Pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:173-174

responseSet.title: Through the Night: Helping Parents and Sleepless Infants. By Dylis Dawsa London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 paperback. 274 Pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Through the Night: Helping Parents and Sleepless Infants. By Dylis Dawsa London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 paperback. 274 Pp.

Veronica Gore

Veronica Gore

Veronica Gore  

This is much more than a book about children with sleeping problems. In it Mrs Daws, an experienced child psychotherapist, combines an analysis of research findings

173

with a psychoanalytic understanding of infant development, and a sympathetic feel for the distress and desperation evoked in parents by a sleepless child. It is divided into three parts. The first contains a detailed description, with many case illustrations, of the way she works with parents. This essentially involves helping the parents to connect the difficulties they are having with their child to their own childhood experiences, thus enabling the parents to find a space to think, and to find their own solutions to their child's sleeping problems.

Part II provides a developmental context for thinking about sleeping problems and includes excellent chapters on the emotional and physiological development of the infant. In this section she develops her central hypothesis that underlying many sleeping problems are issues to do with separation and attachment. The section also includes chapters on the functions of normal and REM (dreaming) sleep, and the way in which the parents handling of the child crucially influences the establishment of sleep patterns.

Part III explores in more depth and through the use of case material the link between the parents' own childhood experiences and the sleeping problems they encounter with their child. The final chapters consider how other factors — single parenthood, working parents, mother's depression — may interact with the infant's sleeping patterns.

Overall, this is an excellent book. It would be of value, not only to child psychotherapists but also to anyone working in the child-health field — Child Psychiatrists, Health Visitors, General Practitioners, Clinical and Educational Psychologists, Social Workers — who in the course of their work encounter ‘sleepless crying infants' and their parents.

174

responseSet.title: Initiating and Developing a Psychoanalytic Approach to Children
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Initiating and Developing a Psychoanalytic Approach to Children**

Adrian Sutton

Adrian Sutton

90 Woodford Road
Bramhall, Cheshire, SK7 1PB

Adrian Sutton  

A description is given of issues encountered in providing a psychoanalytic approach to supervision of individual therapeutic work with children where this was not previously available. Using examples from supervision and treatment, I describe ways in which the manifestations of transference and countertransference can be explained and shown to be useful clinically. The anxiety generated in giving up previous approaches and developing new ones has to be contained in supervision, particularly when therapists are working with seriously damaged children in poorly-resourced services. With specific reference to children, two issues are highlighted: (a) the potential for ‘developmental resonance’ between the child's/adolescent's situation and the trainee/trainer dyad which may lead to enactment in supervision; and (b) the relatively common occurrence of therapy not developing beyond the stage of mutual idealisation’. For some children this may be sufficient but it is suggested that for those with more severe difficulties this may compound their problems. This is highlighted as an area which requires further research in relation to choice of therapists and assessment for therapy. A detailed description is given of a supervision which is functioning well and facilitating therapy. Reference is also made to the experience of becoming a supervisor, with particular emphasis on the need to re-negotiate ‘the capacity to be alone’ as described by Winnicott.

Initiating and developing a psychoanalytical approach to children is a complicated process which makes considerable demands on both supervisor and supervisee. The benefits of attempting it are seen in the developmental opportunities which accrue to the supervisor, supervisee and children with whom they are involved.

responseSet.documentRef: Cawthra, R. (1991) The Psychoanalytic Core. Essays in honor of Leo Rangell. Edited By H. Blum, E. Weinshel & F.R. Rodman International Universities Press $60 hardback. Pp. 536.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:272-272
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Cawthra, R. (1991) The Psychoanalytic Core. Essays in honor of Leo Rangell. Edited By H. Blum, E. Weinshel & F.R. Rodman International Universities Press $60 hardback. Pp. 536.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:272-272
responseSet.documentRefXML: Cawthra, R. (1991) The Psychoanalytic Core. Essays in honor of Leo Rangell. Edited By H. Blum, E. Weinshel & F.R. Rodman International Universities Press $60 hardback. Pp. 536.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:272-272
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 3 272-272 A The Psychoanalytic Core. Essays in honor of Leo Rangell. Edited By H. Blum, E. Weinshel & F.R. Rodman International Universities Press $60 hardback. Pp. 536. Richard Cawthra
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Core. Essays in honor of Leo Rangell. Edited By H. Blum, E. Weinshel & F.R. Rodman International Universities Press $60 hardback. Pp. 536.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Core. Essays in honor of Leo Rangell. Edited By H. Blum, E. Weinshel & F.R. RodmanInternational Universities Press $60 hardback. Pp. 536.

Richard Cawthra

Richard Cawthra

Richard Cawthra  

This book is one of considerable substance, and to be highly recommended to anyone who regards himself as an ongoing student of the psychoanalytic perspective.

Written in honour of Leo Rangell, it comprises a collection of twenty-six chapters by leading analysts such as Charles Brenner, Robert S. Wallerstein, and Jacob A. Arlow principally from the United States, but also from Britain, including Adam Limentani, Pearl King, and Clifford Yorke.

Unlike many other books which are compilations of ‘papers' many of which are already familiar to the reader, this book is brimming with new and fresh contributions which together convey a breadth and depth of analytical thinking associated with the work of Leo Rangell.

If there is a theme to the book it is the superego and its role in the development of mental health and integrity.

For those readers who are steeped in the more interactional and object-related approaches to psychoanalysis, this book would be a refreshing balance. It contains much that stems from the classical Freudian tradition, with an emphasis on a structural model, though applied and extended beyond any narrow confines. I particularly enjoyed Jacob Arlow's chapter on ‘The Quest for Morality’ and Pearl King's chapter entitled ‘On being a Psychoanalyst: Integrity and Vulnerability in Psychoanalytic Organisations'. There are valuable chapters on ‘Thinking and the Superego’ (by Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel) and a ‘Theoretical Frame for the Study of Sexual Perversions' (by Otto Kernberg), which should be helpful for students of psychoanalytic psychotherapy as they both provide such clear frameworks for learning.

Those readers interested in wider applications of psychoanalytic thought will find chapters relevant to childhood trauma, ethics, and the political process.

Owing to its style and layout, this book can be dipped into over a period of time. It contains so much variety while being a book of real depth. It ought to be an essential purchase for everyone's library, whether personal or departmental.

responseSet.title: Evaluation of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy with Children: Therapists' Assessments and Predictions
responseSet.authorMast: Dora Lush,  Mary Boston and  Eve Grainger
responseSet.authorCitation: Lush, D., Boston, M. & Grainger, E.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Evaluation of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy with Children: Therapists' Assessments and Predictions

Dora Lush , Mary Boston and Eve Grainger

Eve Grainger

120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA

Eve Grainger  

Meaningful and clinically relevant evaluation of psychoanalytical psychotherapy requires new models for research. Such evaluation needs to take account of the complexities of personality and inner-world change as well as the more simply assessed changes in external behaviour.

A pilot study to develop a suitable methodology is described. These preliminary results are on a consecutive series of twenty in care and adopted children entering psychotherapy at the Tavistock Clinic. The project evolved from a previous study of psychotherapy with severely deprived children (Boston & Szur 1983), but the method could be used with any group of children in psychoanalytic psychotherapy, and possibly also, in an adapted form, with adults.

Therapists' initial aims and predictions are compared with later assessments of both external, and a wide range of internal, changes. Methods of validation are discussed and case-illustrations given.

responseSet.title: Training Nurses in Psychotherapeutic Skills
responseSet.authorMast: Patricia M Hughes and  Christine Halek
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Training Nurses in Psychotherapeutic Skills

Patricia Hughes MRCPsych and Christine Halek RMN

Christine Halek

Blackshaw Road London SW17 0QT

Christine Halek  

Psychiatric nurses are subjected to considerable stress in working with highly disturbed patients. Both nurses and patients will benefit if nurses have a better understanding of the psychodynamics of the interaction.

Managers in the Mental Health Unit at St George's Hospital were aware of low morale and high levels of stress among nurses and invited a psychotherapeutic input for nurses. A one-year course was set up to introduce nurses to psychodynamic principles. This paper includes a description of the course and an evaluation of the effect of the experience on the nurses' work with patients in general psychiatry.

Results show that the amount of individual and group work done with psychiatric patients does not change. There are significant changes in the nurses' confidence, competence, and understanding of their work.

responseSet.documentRefXML: Schachter, J. (1991) The Significance of Infant Observational Research for Clinical Work with Children, Adolescents and Adults. Edited By Scott Dowling Arnold Rothstein Madison: Int. Univ. Press. $32.50 257 pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:172-172
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 2 172-172 A The Significance of Infant Observational Research for Clinical Work with Children, Adolescents and Adults. Edited By Scott Dowling Arnold Rothstein Madison: Int. Univ. Press. $32.50 257 pp. Joan Schachter
responseSet.title: The Significance of Infant Observational Research for Clinical Work with Children, Adolescents and Adults. Edited By Scott Dowling Arnold Rothstein Madison: Int. Univ. Press. $32.50 257 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Significance of Infant Observational Research for Clinical Work with Children, Adolescents and Adults. Edited By Scott DowlingArnold Rothstein Madison: Int. Univ. Press. $32.50 257 pp.

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter  

Infant-observation research has been making much more of an impact on psychoanalysis in recent years. It seems that researchers and clinicians are more willing to listen and talk to each other. The empirical researchers are able to carry out increasingly sophisticated research on mother-infant interactions, which enables the clinicians to see more clearly the links with their work. This book bears evidence of this interchange, focusing particularly on the effects of infant research on individual psychoanalysts' clinical work and theoretical thinking.

It is the fifth in a series of workshops sponsored by the American Psychoanalytic Association. Overall it gives some indication of changes in psychoanalytic theory and technique in the USA, as well as raising many interesting questions about theory which the empirical research provokes.

Following an introduction and overview by Phyllis Tyson, there are eight clinical accounts and four discussion papers. There is a wealth of material here. The clinical papers present a variety of views on the influence of infant-observation research which are thoroughly discussed in the papers by Morton Shane, Harold Blum, William Meissner and Leo Rangell. Important issues are raised — the continuity versus discontinuity in development debate, the translation of bio-behavioural studies into the psychological realm, the relationship of normal to pathological development, nature-nurture effects on development.

What these papers demonstrate is the useful effect of research in provoking further questions about existing theory, and the direction of future research. For example, do certain aspects of the relationship between the caregiver and child express themselves continually throughout development? The need to distinguish phase-specific strains from continuous strain effects. And overall, the necessity to distinguish clearly the different conceptual frameworks.

There are some cautionary remarks from Tyson and Rangell on the dangers of new research findings leading either to a throwing out of old ideas or the creation of a divergent stream, neither of which leads to the possibility of a real synthesis.

responseSet.documentRef: Malcolm, M. (1991) Too Long a Child: The Mother-Daughter Dyad. By Nina Herman London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 358 pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:173-173
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Malcolm, M. (1991) Too Long a Child: The Mother-Daughter Dyad. By Nina Herman London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 358 pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:173-173
responseSet.documentRefXML: Malcolm, M. (1991) Too Long a Child: The Mother-Daughter Dyad. By Nina Herman London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 358 pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:173-173
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 2 173-173 A Too Long a Child: The Mother-Daughter Dyad. By Nina Herman London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 358 pp. Margaret Malcolm
responseSet.title: Too Long a Child: The Mother-Daughter Dyad. By Nina Herman London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 358 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Too Long a Child: The Mother-Daughter Dyad. By Nina Herman London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 358 pp.

Margaret Malcolm

Margaret Malcolm

Margaret Malcolm  

Nine Herman's book explores the ambivalence of the mother-daughter relationship: the struggle by mothers and daughters to be free and autonomous versus the longing for and fear of symbiotic union.

Using examples from myth and literature as well as real women from Mediaeval times to the present day, Herman reveals the universality of this conflict throughout the ages.

The author takes an optimistic view. She sees a gradual resolution with each generation contributing to the freedom of the next. In our own society she sees the most hope and attributes this both to the greater opportunity for women to develop outside the home and the greater availability of the father, who will help both wife and daughter with the task of separation.

If at times the language is rather flowery and the theme repetitive it is nonetheless a fascinating book and a considerable achievement. It is not a psychoanalytic textbook, although Herman uses psychoanalytic insights to develop her theme. It will be read by anyone who has an interest in women and their struggle for independence.

173

responseSet.title: The Patient, the Nurse, his Life and Her Mother: Psychodynamic Influences in Nurse Education and Practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Patient, the Nurse, his Life and Her Mother: Psychodynamic Influences in Nurse Education and Practice**

Harry Wright

Harry Wright

Gwinllan Y Maes Rhayader Powys LD6 5DE

Harry Wright  

Using the film The Cook, the Thief, His Wife and her Lover (from which this paper's title is borrowed) as a powerful illustration, the paper examines some of the unconscious processes which form part of everyday life for nurses working in the community, particularly on the wards of our hospitals. It is an attempt to describe some of these processes and to understand the conditions that promote them. With acknowledgements to the pioneers in this sort of work, such as Main, Menzies-Lyth, Trist, Bamforth and Jaques, the paper looks at the ethology of institutions and at activity that takes place on the boundaries between self and other, between the inner world and external reality, and between various levels in the hierarchy.

The paper identifies some of the ‘routines' which grew up in nursing (and those which have replaced them), the unconscious purpose of which is to safeguard the emotional and psychological integrity of the nurse, through creating a distance between herself and her patients. The paper argues against removing these necessary defences until more helpful alternatives are in place. To fulfil this aim, it recommends that clinical supervision and support seminars should become a regular part of all nurse-training programmes, and should continue throughout the professional life of the nurse.

responseSet.documentRef: Crick, P. (1991) Understanding women in distress By Pamela Ashurst and Zaida Hall with contributions from others. London: Routledge, £25 hardback, £12.95 paperback 237 Pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:174-175
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Crick, P. (1991) Understanding women in distress By Pamela Ashurst and Zaida Hall with contributions from others. London: Routledge, £25 hardback, £12.95 paperback 237 Pp.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:174-175

responseSet.title: Understanding women in distress By Pamela Ashurst and Zaida Hall with contributions from others. London: Routledge, £25 hardback, £12.95 paperback 237 Pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding women in distress By Pamela Ashurst and Zaida Hall with contributions from others. London: Routledge, £25 hardback, £12.95 paperback 237 Pp.

Penelope Crick

Penelope Crick

Penelope Crick  

There are many sources of distress for women that are specifically addressed in this book. A major concern is with loss and the many ways in which a woman can experience this, whether through infertility, miscarriage, having a damaged baby, death of a child, death of a partner, divorce, disfigurement, hysterectomy, and mastectomy. There are also chapters on disturbances of psychosexual development related to eating disorders, lesbianism, childlessness, and situations where woman are the victims, as well as the perpetrators, of abuse and perversion. There are also chapters concerned with particular aspects of mothering, such as maternal depression, step-parenting, and being a single mother.

The authors' intention is to produce a book concerned with the distress of women that will raise awareness of women's needs.

174

responseSet.title: Ethical Dilemmas for Nurses and Their Emotional Implications
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ethical Dilemmas for Nurses and Their Emotional Implications**

Lawrence Goldie

Lawrence Goldie

23 Harley Street London WIN IDA

Lawrence Goldie  

Some of the ethical and emotional problems that are presented to nurses are described. Nurses dealing with them are often the same age as medical students who are just starting their training and on qualifying as doctors will be less experienced in dealing with various life-and-death dilemmas than the nurses looking after their patients. It is contended that despite the fact that almost everyone at some time in their lives is going to depend upon a nurse, the subject of nursing and its dilemmas has been largely neglected, particularly by psychoanalytic/psychotherapy training organisations. Training is described which it is hoped would remedy some of the deficiencies of current nurse-training and encourage personal development and growth. It is also suggested that the training of medical students should include a period of nurse experience and training. The power which can be invested in the nurse is also described, along with the corrupting effect this can have on the nurse/patient relationship.

responseSet.documentRef: Oswald, I. (1991) Dynamics of the Social — Essays By Isabel Menzies-Lyth London: Free Association Books £ 8.95 Pp. 274. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:272-273
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Oswald, I. (1991) Dynamics of the Social — Essays By Isabel Menzies-Lyth London: Free Association Books £ 8.95 Pp. 274. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:272-273
responseSet.documentRefXML: Oswald, I. (1991) Dynamics of the Social — Essays By Isabel Menzies-Lyth London: Free Association Books £ 8.95 Pp. 274. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:272-273
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 3 272-273 B Dynamics of the Social — Essays By Isabel Menzies-Lyth London: Free Association Books £ 8.95 Pp. 274 Iain Oswald
responseSet.title: Dynamics of the Social — Essays By Isabel Menzies-Lyth London: Free Association Books £ 8.95 Pp. 274
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dynamics of the Social — Essays By Isabel Menzies-Lyth London: Free Association Books £ 8.95 Pp. 274

Iain Oswald

Iain Oswald

Iain Oswald  

This book is the second volume of Isabel Menzies Lyth's selected essays. Unlike Volume 1, many of these papers have not been previously published. This is mostly because the field-research papers derive from confidential reports to clients that have been purposely re-written for this volume. This makes the book very interesting

272

to the clinician who is unfamiliar with the field of institutional consultancy, as well as to the expert. The material about the application of psychoanalytic theories of anxiety and unconscious processes applied to such ordinary human activities as road-safety and motorcycling or eating ice-cream and chocolate makes thought-provoking reading. Menzies Lyth succeeds in offering up these areas to psychological inquiry and throwing light on their deeper meaning. At times, I felt she pushed the facts somewhat to fit her theoretical view of a topic, as, for example, in the paper on motorcycling.

One can sense her consultation style from the lucid and straightforward way that she writes — encouraging staff to talk freely and raise questions that concern them in their organisations. In this way she helped the client institution tackle its problems by drawing their attention to the latent content that lies behind the manifest complaints and disruptions that beset their institution.

The first three papers are a statement of Menzies Lyth's theoretical views of social institutions, drawing extensively on Bion's thinking about groups, but also influenced by social science theory. In addition to the papers on pleasure foods and road-safety is one on family breakdown in urban society. Then three papers which describe work with large national organisations: the Fire Brigade, a group of mental hospitals, and local-authority day-care facilities. Here Menzies Lyth shows how, when the diagnosis had been made, the real problem lay in getting the institution to take seriously the required treatment; she thus draws attention to the limitations of consultancy work when dealing with the inertia of large institutions.

responseSet.documentRef: Oswald, I. (1991) Recovery from Schizophrenia: Psychiatry and Political Economy By Richard Warner. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul. £9.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:273-274
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Oswald, I. (1991) Recovery from Schizophrenia: Psychiatry and Political Economy By Richard Warner. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul. £9.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:273-274
responseSet.documentRefXML: Oswald, I. (1991) Recovery from Schizophrenia: Psychiatry and Political Economy By Richard Warner. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul. £9.95.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:273-274
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 3 273-274 Recovery from Schizophrenia: Psychiatry and Political Economy By Richard Warner. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul. £9.95. Iain Oswald
responseSet.title: Recovery from Schizophrenia: Psychiatry and Political Economy By Richard Warner. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul. £9.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Recovery from Schizophrenia: Psychiatry and Political Economy By Richard Warner. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul. £9.95.

Iain Oswald

Iain Oswald

Iain Oswald  

This book was originally published in the USA in 1985 in hardback, and was hailed as a substantial contribution to the social psychiatry of schizophrenia. Its success has led to this paperback edition. The author is an English-trained psychiatrist working in the United States.

It is a masterly review of the whole field of schizophrenia: its history, aetiology, treatment, and particularly the vexed issue of recovery, and what factors seem to

273

determine recovery. The book's subtitle, ‘Psychiatry and Political Economy’, hints at where the author believes the key to understanding the poor outcome of schizophrenia lies. He believes that unemployment and the state of the economy have a profound effect on the prevalence and outcome of the disease. During economic recessions there is an increase in incidence of the disease and a reduction in recovery rates. Warner is not saying that there is a simple causal link, but that such factors mould the course of the disease and influence its outcome substantially; and that this factor is all too often neglected by the psychiatric profession in favour of biological, genetic, or psychological factors. He quotes many substantial studies to support this view.

Warner draws our attention to the importance of remembering that between twenty-five and forty per cent of schizophrenics have a potentially good prognosis, and that social, community, and labour factors will determine whether this group will do well or not. Cross-cultural studies show convincing evidence that despite the multi-billion-dollar investment in the treatment of schizophrenia in the West, outcome is better in the Third World, and this is with negligible expenditure. He argues that this is to do with the support offered by extended-family networks and the less intense pressure of the wage economy.

Warner also shows some interesting studies that support the view that in good-prognosis schizophrenia, anti-psychotic medication can worsen the prognosis. His model for the rehabilitation of schizophrenics is based on the ideas of William Tuke and the York Retreat of the eighteenth century. Application of this model today would mean a properly-funded community programme based on small treatment units that pay attention to the employment and family networks with which the recovering schizophrenic has to cope.

responseSet.title: Humour in Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Humour in Psychotherapy

David Mann

David Mann

Flat 6 164 Lordship Road London N16 5HB

David Mann  

This paper examines some of the uses of humour in psychotherapy. Literature is reviewed and clinical examples given to illustrate how patients and therapists may be affected by humour. The conclusion reached is that using humour does not make our work less serious, but may in fact help the patient to become more integrated and more spontaneous in his relationships.

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 2 171-171 Psychic Equilibrium and Psychic Change. Selected Papers of Betty Joseph. Edited by Elizabeth Bott Spillius and Michael Feldman. No.9 in The New Library of Psycho-Analysis series. General Editor: David Tuckett. London: Routledge & Institute of Psycho-Analysis. £29.95 hardback £14.95 paperback. Pp. 230. Penelope Crick
responseSet.title: Psychic Equilibrium and Psychic Change. Selected Papers of Betty Joseph. Edited by Elizabeth Bott Spillius and Michael Feldman. No.9 in The New Library of Psycho-Analysis series. General Editor: David Tuckett. London: Routledge & Institute of Psycho-Analysis. £29.95 hardback £14.95 paperback. Pp. 230.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Equilibrium and Psychic Change. Selected Papers of Betty Joseph. Edited by Elizabeth Bott Spillius and Michael Feldman. No.9 in The New Library of Psycho-Analysis series. General Editor: David Tuckett. London: Routledge & Institute of Psycho-Analysis. £29.95 hardback £14.95 paperback. Pp. 230.

Penelope Crick

Penelope Crick

Penelope Crick  

This is a comprehensive collection of nearly all the paper written by Betty Joseph over the last thirty years. The fifteen papers, some published here for the first time, are divided by the editors into four sections, each section marking a particular stage in the development of her thinking and the corresponding implications for technique.

Betty Joseph is well known, perhaps in some quarters to the point of a caricature that obscures the value of the ideas, for her belief that attention to what goes on in the analytic session is of greater importance and value than a pursuit of the ‘meaning’ of the patient's verbal material. In the moment-to-moment shifts in transference and countertransference lies the richest source of information about the patient's internal world, his object relationships and history. With vivid clinical illustrations, Betty Joseph shows how she observes the tenacity with which, despite the conscious wish to change, patients maintain and enact their ‘psychic equilibrium’, their constellation of defences against anxiety. She shows how the analyst can get drawn into the particular system, for example, by projective identification, to prevent the equilibrium being disturbed. Psychic change is made possible when, through careful and consistent attention to the transference and countertransference in the analytic situation, the analyst can experience and recognise the pressure he is put under without acting it out, and then, with the patient, arrive at a basic understanding of what goes on.

The Editors have provided a general introduction to Betty Joseph's work as well as brief introductions to each section of the book. These are beautifully written, lucid essays that provide a valuable framework for the reader. Not only do they place Betty Joseph's work within developments in psychoanalysis and Kleinian theory, but they succeed in making the more complex papers more accessible.

If you have found the work, or the caricature, too forbidding to try, this book will be a great help if you wish to make a foray into the work of this influential analyst. Those already familiar with her work will find it valuable to have this collection of papers; and the editorial contributions and preface by Hanna Segal will be an interesting way into a further study of the development of Betty Joseph's ideas.

responseSet.title: Good Supervision: On the Experience of being Supervised
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Good Supervision: On the Experience of being Supervised**

Penelope Crick

Penelope Crick

41 Princess May Road London N16 8DF

Penelope Crick  

This paper was written for presentation at a symposium of trainers when the author was a trainee in psychoanalytical psychotherapy. The original title was ‘Good Supervision: a consumer's guide’. The author drew not only on her own experience of being supervised but also on that of colleagues in training. It was found that it was essential to conceptualise the experience in terms of the triangular relationship between student, patient, and supervisor. Primitive feelings are evoked by the supervisory process and some of these are described. Characteristics of supervisors and the supervisory process and how these are felt to help, or otherwise, are discussed.

responseSet.documentRef: Gore, V. (1991) Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior By John D. Sutherland London: Free Association Books.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:271-271
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Gore, V. (1991) Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior By John D. Sutherland London: Free Association Books.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:271-271
responseSet.documentRefXML: Gore, V. (1991) Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior By John D. Sutherland London: Free Association Books.. Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 5:271-271
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 1991 5 3 271-271 Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior By John D. Sutherland London: Free Association Books. Veronica Gore
responseSet.title: Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior By John D. Sutherland London: Free Association Books.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior By John D. Sutherland London: Free Association Books.

Veronica Gore

Veronica Gore

Veronica Gore  

This book, written by John Sutherland, a ‘pupil, colleague and friend’ of Ronald Fairbairn, is an informative and fascinating psychobiography of Fairbairn. It takes the reader on two, linked, journeys into the interior of the human mind. One charts very carefully the development of Fairbairn's theoretical contribution, tracing the evolution of themes through the chronological study of his published papers. Sutherland portrays very clearly the main thesis of Fairbairn's theoretical developments in psychoanalytic thinking: that at the core of the personality, and that at the base of all psychopathology lies a split in the ego, a psychic rupture, of what was to begin with a unitary ego. This split in the self finds its genesis in ‘an innate rejection of what brought pain and thus had to be avoided’, and was due, in Fairbairn's view, to early maternal and environmental failure. Since frustration of oral and dependency needs is inevitable, this split in the ego is at the core of everyone's personality. For Fairbairn, the development of the internal world, and the conflict between love and hate, are primarily related to the quality of early environmental provision. In this way he came to the central tenet of his theory: that libido is essentially object-seeking rather than pleasure-seeking.

The other inner world the book explores is that of Fairbairn himself. Sutherland drawn on biographical detail, Fairbairn's self-analytic notes, and his personal knowledge of the man, to give a detailed reconstruction of Fairbairn's inner world. The central chapters of the book are a thoughtful and sensitive exploration of the interplay between Fairbairn's inner world and his major theoretical constructs.

Sutherland carefully shows why and where Fairbairn departs from classical Freudian thinking, and also the impact of Mrs Klein's paper on him, and on the evolution of his ideas.

Overall, the book is an informative and scholarly work concerning itself both with Fairbairn's life and with the development of his distinctive contribution to psychoanalytic thinking. It is clearly not an objective critique of Fairbairn's ideas — Sutherland early on in the book declares himself a friend. However, it is an interesting and informative read, and would be of interest to anyone studying the development of psychoanalytic theory. It places Fairbairn firmly in a historical context with Freud and Klein, and brings his specific contribution from its relative obscurity more clearly into the light of day.

responseSet.title: Wildest Dreams: Aspiration, Identification, and Symbol-Formation in Depressed Children
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wildest Dreams: Aspiration, Identification, and Symbol-Formation in Depressed Children**

Anne Alvarez

Anne Alvarez

120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA

Anne Alvarez  

This paper explores some technical and theoretical implications of psychotherapy with deprived and severely depressed children. Phantasies about the future, and dreams of nobility, are seen to play a vitalising part in normal development. Grandiose lies may be similar to, but far more tentative and communicative than, the declarative communications of the manic child. The phantasies may sometimes need to emerge first in the mind of the therapist through a process which may be termed ‘anticipatory identification’. The need for a grammar of such communicative identification processes is discussed; so also are implications for processes of symbol-formation.

<artinfo arttype="ART" j="PPTX" ISSN="0266-8734" id="PPTX.005.0261A">
<artyear>1991</artyear>

responseSet.title: Reading between the Lines: The Child's Fear of Meaning
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading between the Lines: The Child's Fear of Meaning**

Mia Beaumont

Mia Beaumont

Mia Beaumont  

This paper explores the emotional factors that lie behind a child's failure to read. My observations are based on work as an educational therapist employed in a child guidance unit. In trying to understand the reasons for the child's neurotic reluctance to learn, the following sources have proved helpful: Klein on envy; Bowlby on the effect on the child of a relationship with an untrustworthy adult; and Bettelheim's Learning to Read. In conclusion some ways are examined in which the child can be helped to overcome his fear of the meaning of words.
